one_line_takeaway: SERSEM improves white-box membership inference against code LLMs by roughly 20 AUC-ROC points over probability-based baselines (0.79 vs. up to 0.59) by re-weighting scores toward AST/linter/spellcheck-flagged 'human anomaly' tokens (comments, long identifiers, formatting errors) instead of treating code like natural-language text.
key_finding: SERSEM achieves an overall AUC-ROC of 0.7913 on StarCoder2-3B and 0.7867 on StarCoder2-7B, versus baseline overall AUC-ROC (3B) of Loss=0.5881, Min-K% Prob=0.5931, and PAC=0.4712 -- an improvement of over 20 percentage points over the strongest baseline. The gap holds across all five languages (Table 2 per-language AUC-ROC, SERSEM 3B / 7B): Go 0.8121/0.8071, Java 0.7738/0.7632, Python 0.8066/0.8045, Ruby 0.8155/0.8151, Rust 0.7402/0.7379 (SERSEM's lowest score, still well above baselines). Ruby and Go scored highest, which the authors attribute to those languages' flexible, less rigidly typed syntax allowing more expression of 'human coding chaos'; Rust and Java scored lowest due to stricter structural/typed boilerplate that suppresses idiosyncratic human variation.
core_contribution: The paper introduces SERSEM (Selective Entropy-Weighted Scoring for Membership Inference), a white-box membership inference attack (MIA) against code language models, submitted as a solution to the Poisoned Chalice Competition 2026. Rather than relying on raw sequence-level output probabilities (as prior baselines do), SERSEM builds a continuous, character-level 'anomaly weight mask' via static AST analysis (tree-sitter), offline linting (flake8), and spellchecking-based multilingual/naming irregularity detection (PyEnchant). This mask assigns higher weight to human-centric coding anomalies (long identifiers, string literals, linter formatting errors, comments/multilingual slippage, 'TODO/FIXME' tags) and downweights predictable syntactic boilerplate (weights given explicitly in Table 1: boilerplate 0.1, standard identifiers 1.0, long identifiers 3.0, literal strings 5.0, linter errors 5.0, comments/multilingual slippage 10.0, psychological tags 10.0). These heuristic weights are projected onto subword tokens and used in two ways: (1) to calibrate a sigmoid-transformed Z-score of the target token's output logit relative to the full vocabulary, and (2) to weight-pool internal transformer activations extracted via forward hooks at every layer (integrating the LUMIA method, which the authors cite as prior work, not their own invention) into an augmented activation space. A lightweight MLP probe is trained via binary cross-entropy on these pooled activations from the top-5 most discriminative layers (selected using shadow validation data), and its ensembled sigmoid outputs form the final membership confidence score in [0,1].
threat_model: White-box membership inference attacker with full internal access to a target code LLM (parameters, output logits, and ability to attach forward hooks to extract intermediate-layer activations during a no-gradient forward pass). The adversary's goal is to determine whether a specific source-code sample was part of the model's training set, in order to detect data contamination (e.g., verbatim memorization of non-permissively licensed or proprietary code). This is the evaluation framework defined by the Poisoned Chalice Competition 2026, using StarCoder2-3B and StarCoder2-7B (both pretrained on The Stack v2) as target models, with The Stack v2 samples as verified training members and The Heap dataset as non-members.
limitations: The paper itself acknowledges: (1) hardware-constrained validation -- all reported experiments used float16 on a single TITAN RTX rather than the bfloat16 intended for the competition's A100 hardware, so PAC (and possibly others) were run under mismatched precision from their standard configuration; (2) heuristic weights in Table 1 (e.g., 0.1/1.0/3.0/5.0/10.0) appear to be manually fixed rather than learned or tuned per target distribution -- the authors list 'automatically tuning heuristic weights per target distribution' as future work; (3) non-Python languages rely on generic regex fallbacks rather than native linters, also flagged as future work; (4) the internal-activation component is a direct application of the pre-existing LUMIA method (Ibanez-Lissen et al., ESORICS 2025) rather than a novel contribution of this paper -- SERSEM's novelty lies specifically in the AST/linting/spellcheck-derived anomaly weighting scheme and its fusion with LUMIA-style probing, not in activation probing itself; (5) evaluation is confined to two model sizes of one model family (StarCoder2) and a single competition dataset split, so generalization to other code LLM architectures or contamination scenarios is untested within the paper.

datasets_used: The Stack v2 (member/training-data source for StarCoder2)
Poisoned Chalice Competition 2026 development dataset (25,000-sample balanced split across Python, Java, Go, Ruby, Rust)
quantitative_results: Baseline overall AUC-ROC on 3B: Loss 0.5881, Min-K% Prob 0.5931, PAC 0.4712
Per-language AUC-ROC (SERSEM 3B / 7B): Go 0.8121/0.8071, Java 0.7738/0.7632, Python 0.8066/0.8045, Ruby 0.8155/0.8151 (best), Rust 0.7402/0.7379 (worst)

Token weighting scheme (Table 1): boilerplate/syntax 0.1, standard identifiers (<10 chars) 1.0, long identifiers (>=10 chars) 3.0, literal strings 5.0, linter formatting errors 5.0, comments & multilingual slippage 10.0, psychological tags (TODO) 10.0
